title: Resetless Valid-Masked Register File
motivation:
The current best core already exceeds the CoreMark throughput target
substantially: 577.76 iter/s versus the required 400 iter/s. The failing
axis is area, with 5944 LUT4 against the 3500 LUT4 target, so this
micro_opt slot should target a localized LUT/FF reduction that does not
change the pipeline schedule.

The register file is still a 32 x 32 array that synchronously resets every
physical data bit. That reset loop prevents the cheapest resetless LUTRAM
mapping and adds reset muxing/fanout in the decode read region. Software and
the existing tests only require nonzero registers to read as zero after
reset until written; a small valid mask can provide that behavior while the
data payload itself becomes resetless.
hypothesis:
Replace the reset-cleared register data array in `rtl/reg_file.sv` with
resetless storage plus a resettable valid mask for x1 through x31. On reset,
clear only the valid bits. On a write to a nonzero architectural register,
update the resetless data payload and set the corresponding valid bit. Reads
preserve the current externally visible behavior: x0 always returns zero,
same-cycle writeback to a read address returns `w_data`, valid nonzero
registers return stored data, and invalid nonzero registers return zero.

Implement the storage in a synthesis-friendly shape for the FPGA, for
example two duplicated resetless read banks with one synchronous write to
both banks, so the two combinational read ports can map to distributed RAM
instead of resettable flip-flops. Avoid adding a subtractor or broad decode
on the read path; the valid mask should be indexed directly by the
architectural register number with x0 handled as a special zero case.

This changes no ISA behavior, forwarding priority, load-use interlock,
branch prediction, divider protocol, memory ordering, RVFI field, retirement
order, or top-level IO. CoreMark cycles should be identical. The intended
win is a large LUT4 and FF/reset-fanout reduction on the area axis, with
fitness staying within the current throughput headroom even if the resetless
RAM read path costs a little Fmax.

changes.description:
Remove the reset loop over the 32 x 32 data payload. Add a resettable
valid mask for nonzero registers and keep the data arrays resetless,
optionally as two mirrored read banks to encourage distributed-RAM
inference for the two asynchronous read ports. Preserve the module
interface, x0 hardwiring, nonzero write gating, and write-first bypass
behavior. Reads must check x0 and same-cycle writeback before consulting
the valid mask; invalid nonzero reads return zero without exposing
uninitialized resetless payload bits.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What Changed

- Reworked `rtl/reg_file.sv` so reset clears only a 32-bit architectural valid mask.
- Made the register data payload resetless and duplicated it into two mirrored read banks, one per combinational read port.
- Writes to nonzero architectural registers update both payload banks and set `valid_q[w_addr]`.
- Reads still prioritize x0, then same-cycle writeback bypass, then valid stored data; invalid nonzero registers return zero without exposing resetless payload bits.

## Deviations

- I used a 32-bit valid mask indexed directly by architectural register number. Bit 0 is never set because x0 writes are dropped and x0 reads return zero before the valid check. This avoids subtract/index remapping on the read path at the cost of one unused valid bit.
- Added a `ram_style = "distributed"` attribute on both read banks as a synthesis hint; the implementation does not rely on the attribute for correctness.

## Concerns

- No known functional concerns. The change is limited to the register file and keeps the module interface, write-first bypass, x0 behavior, pipeline schedule, and RVFI-visible values unchanged.
- A direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` in this worktree failed before generating checks because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected shared checkout and the script could not remove staged files there (`Operation not permitted`). I ran the same script from a writable `/tmp/rf_formal.NTpDRb` mirror with the current RTL symlinked in.

## Local Status

- Lint: pass, `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`.
- Focused unit test: pass, `pytest -q test/test_reg_file.py`.
- Formal: pass from the writable mirror, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Remove IF replay table
motivation: LUT4 is the only failing axis: the current design reaches 577.76 iter/s against a 400 iter/s CoreMark target, but uses 5944 LUT4 against a 3500 LUT4 target. The largest clearly structural area feature in the RTL is the two-bank 128-entry instruction replay table in IF. It stores instructions, tags, valid bits, MRU state, and hit/bypass comparators to mask random imem stalls, even though the current CoreMark margin is large enough to trade some stall-hiding for a major area reduction.
hypothesis: Replace the IF replay-table front end with a bare PC fetch stage that keeps the existing static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction but treats io_imemReady=0 as a normal fetch stall. Removing the replay arrays and their lookup/fill control should cut a large block of LUTRAM/FF/comparator logic and may also improve Fmax by shortening IF-side control. CoreMark/MHz will drop because random imem stalls are no longer hidden, but the baseline has enough performance headroom that the design should remain above the 400 iter/s target while moving LUT4 much closer to the 3500 target.

changes.description: Delete the replay-table arrays, tag/MRU state, lookup/fill/bypass logic, and replay-current-hit path; drive fetch_ready directly from imem_ready while preserving redirect priority, PC hold behavior, NOP-on-flush, and static branch/JAL prediction from the live instruction word.
Update the IF/ID bundle comments to remove replay-table-specific documentation while keeping the predicted_taken and predicted_target fields unchanged.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Replaced the IF replay-table front end with a bare PC fetch stage in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- Removed both replay banks, tag arrays, valid bits, MRU state, lookup/fill control, registered replay candidate state, and bypass logic.
- `fetch_ready` now follows `imem_ready` directly, and `fetch_instr` is the live `imem_data` only when `imem_ready` is high.
- Preserved redirect priority over stalls, PC hold on stalls, invalid NOP output on flush/imem stall, and static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction from the live instruction word.
- Updated replay-specific comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`, `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`, and one stale execute-stage comment.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis. I also removed stale documentation references outside the advisory files so the RTL comments match the new fetch behavior.

## Concerns

- CoreMark/MHz is expected to drop under randomized imem stalls because IF no longer supplies cached instruction words during `io_imemReady=0`.
- The direct worktree invocation of `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not stage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside this sandbox's writable roots. The failure happened before any formal check ran.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked in the worktree by sandbox permissions while removing staged files under the symlinked `formal/riscv-formal`.
- Mirrored the needed formal tree under `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-check`, symlinked in the current `rtl/`, and ran the same `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.
